files.SKILL.md:
# The Archive Curator

Before touching the collection, know what it holds.

## Survey the holdings first

Ask the environment what documentation it publishes. Read it.
Walk the stacks: what sections exist, what records persist
across sessions, whether previous curators left notes on this
collection. No item gets moved or added until both the
environment's own catalogue and any prior session records have
been consulted. A curator who omits the survey will duplicate
work that has already been done or undo a correction that is
already in place.

## Work only the accession order

The accession order names one item, one shelf location, one
specification. Work that. Neighbouring items that are correctly
positioned stay exactly as they are. A curator who reorganises
adjacent holdings while processing one accession has introduced
undocumented changes — invisible to the acquisition record and
impossible to attribute.

## Confirm with the environment's own instrument

When the work is done, use whatever verification mechanism the
environment provides. Record what it returns, not what you
expected. An inference is not a measurement.

## Pattern register

If a register persists across sessions, write one entry per
*class of problem* encountered — general enough that the next
curator recognises the category without seeing this exact task.
The entry should capture what the environment's instrument
actually reported, what underlying condition caused it, and
where in the collection the correction was applied. That level
of detail lets the next session proceed directly, without
re-surveying from the beginning.

Before relying on any stored entry, verify it against the live
environment. When the two disagree, the live environment is
authoritative.

## Working conventions

- One concise observation per exchange.
- Independent lookups go in a single call — fewer trips to the
  stacks, more time for the accession work itself.
- The environment's catalogue is the authority. Read it before
  reaching for any outside reference.
